content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
  
 
March 24, 2020 
 
 
Lisa A. Travis 
County Counsel 
County of Sacramento 
700 H Street, Suite 2650 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
 
Our File No.  A-20-038 
 
Dear Ms. Travis: 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1   
 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
 
QUESTION 
 
 
Under the Act, may Susan Peters, a member of the Sacramento County Board of 
Supervisors, take part in decisions relating to the NewBridge Specific Plan, which would ultimately 
establish a new community and introduce new residential units, commercial space, an elementary 
school, and a fire station, given that Supervisor Peters owns real property and a commercial 
building with tenants located more than 10,000 feet away? 
 
CONCLUSION 
 
 
Yes, given the significant distance between her economic interests and the nearest boundary 
of the NewBridge Specific Plan, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to the 
 
 
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-20-038 
Page No. 2 
 
 
project would have a material financial effect on Supervisor Peters’ real property, business, or 
source-of-income economic interests. 
 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
 
 
The Sacramento County (“County”) Board of Supervisors (“Board”) will soon consider 
approval of the NewBridge Specific Plan (“NSP”), which will include certifying a Final 
Environmental Impact Report, approval of several General Plan amendments, approval of a 
Community Plan Amendment to the Vineyard Community Plan, and approval of a Development 
Agreement and other related actions. 
 
Sacramento Ranch, LLC, the applicant for the NewBridge project, submitted a request to 
initiate the NSP in 2011. In 2012, the Board approved the initiation of the project, consisting of 
adopting resolutions to initiate the master planning process and allow the Assistant County 
Executive Officer to enter into a funding agreement between the County and the applicant. The 
Board also adopted the NewBridge Master Plan Reimbursement Agreement. Later that year, upon 
request, the Board directed staff to begin the environmental review process. 
 
The NSP site is located in the Vineyard community of unincorporated Sacramento County, 
southeast of Mather Airport, and just west of the City of Rancho Cordova. The project is outside the 
County’s Urban Planning Area (“UPA”), but it is within the Urban Services Boundary (“USB”). 
The project will result in the development of approximately 790 acres of the total 1,095.3-acre Plan 
area. 
 
The project applicant is now requesting General Plan Amendments, a Community Plan 
Amendment, Zoning Ordinance Amendments, and the adoption of supporting studies and 
documents. The proposed specific plan includes 3,075 residential units, 500,000 square feet of 
commercial/office uses, an elementary school site, and a fire station site. The project will also 
include open space preserves consistent with the South Sacramento Habitat Conservation Plant, 
parks, landscape corridors, and bike and pedestrian trails throughout the plan area. The new 
community will be served by public streets and provide connections to future development to the 
north. The project also includes an Affordable Housing Strategy, which consists of on-site land 
dedication for the construction of affordable housing units and payment of affordable housing fees.  
The Project will require amendments to the General Plan in order to include the site within 
the County’s UPA and recognize the proposed land uses, streets, and bikeways on the General 
Plan’s Land Use Diagram, Transportation Plan, and Bicycle Master Plan. In the Vineyard 
Community Plan, the entire site will be redesignated from Agriculture and Industrial Uses to the 
NSP Area. A Zoning Ordinance Amendment is also required to include the adoption of the NSP 
including text, a land use diagram, Design Guidelines, and Development Standards. The 
amendment will also include the NewBridge Development Agreement. The adopted NSP will then 
become the primary land use document, which stipulates uses, design guidelines, and development 
standards that are allowable within the Project area. 
The Board will consider creation of the NewBridge Public Facilities Financing Plan, which 
identifies cost estimates and a strategy for financing the infrastructure and public facilities required 


File No. A-20-038 
Page No. 3 
 
 
to serve the Project area. The Board action also contemplates creation of an Urban Services Plan, 
which describes the services required for the project, how they are funded, and demonstrates that 
the project will not adversely affect the County’s fiscal resources. Additionally, the Project will 
require the Board to enter into a Development Agreement with the project applicant, through which 
the applicant will agree to implement several of the obligations to benefit the area. The Board will 
likely consider the NSP Project on May 5, 2020. 
Supervisor Susan Peters has been a member of the Sacramento County Board of Supervisors 
since 2005. Supervisor Peters owns a commercial office building with the State of California, 
Office of Emergency Services, Conduent, Inc., and Educational Credit Management Corporation as 
the building’s current tenants. Supervisor Peters also owns undeveloped land within the City of 
Rancho Cordova. Both real property interests are located in the City of Rancho Cordova, more than 
10,000 feet away from the closest boundary of the NSP area. 
 
ANALYSIS 
 
Section 87100 of the Act provides, “[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest.” “A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family,” or on certain enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) As pertinent to 
the facts provided, those economic interests include: 
 
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(c) Any source of income aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
(Section 87103(a)-(c).) Accordingly, Supervisor Peters has potentially disqualifying economic 
interests in her commercial building as a business entity, her tenants as a source of income, and both 
of her properties as real property interests. 
 
 
Supervisor Peters’ economic interests will not be explicitly involved in any of the NSP 
decisions before the Board. Regulation 18701(b) provides the standard for determining whether a 
financial effect of a governmental decision is reasonably foreseeable where the economic interest is 
not explicitly involved in the decision. That standard provides, “[a] financial effect need not be 
likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized 
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the 
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public 
official’s control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.” 
 


File No. A-20-038 
Page No. 4 
 
 
 
Under Regulation 18702.2(b), “[t]he financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel 
of real property in which an official has a financial interest involving property involving 1,000 feet 
or more from the property line of the official’s property is presumed not to be material. This 
presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the governmental decision would 
have a substantial effect on the official’s property.” 
 
 
With respect to economic interests in business entities, Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) provides 
that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on an official’s financial 
interest in a business entity is material if the decision may result in an increase or decrease of the 
entity’s annual gross revenues, or the value of the entity’s assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to 
or more than $1,000,000 or five percent of the entity’s annual gross revenues and the increase or 
decrease is at least $10,000. A reasonably foreseeable financial effect is also considered material if 
the decision may cause the business entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or 
eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than $250,000 or one percent of the entity’s 
annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).) 
 
 
Regulation 18702.3 provides the standards for determining whether a governmental 
decision’s reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official’s source of income is material. 
 
Here, both of Supervisor Peters’ real property interests are located over 10,000 feet from the 
closest boundary of the NSP area. Per Regulation 18702.2(b), it is presumed the decisions 
pertaining to the NSP would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on 
Supervisor Peters’ real property interests. We note that the establishment of a new community, 
including additional residential units and commercial space, could potentially have a financial effect 
on nearby real property. Based on the facts provided, however, there is no clear and convincing 
evidence rebutting the presumption that there would be no material effect on Supervisor Peters’ 
property located more than 10,000 feet away from the NSP site. (Regulation 18702.2(b).) Similarly, 
although it is possible that the NSP could have a financial effect on nearby businesses, there are no 
facts provided indicating Supervisor Peters’ business entity would be materially affected under the 
standards laid out in Regulation 18702.1(a), as described above.2 
 
 
Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit Supervisor Peters from taking part in NSP decisions 
with respect to her real property and business entity economic interests. However, we note that the 
Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision that would have a 
reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on the official’s source of income, as laid out in 
Regulation 18702.3. While there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the tenants 
resulting from their leasehold interests, no facts have been provided regarding the foreseeable 
financial effects the NSP decisions might have on Supervisor Peter’s tenants resulting from the 
other business interests or property interests the tenants may have. To the extent that a tenant has 
any other interest implicated by the decision, Supervisor Peters may be disqualified from the 
decision and may wish to seek additional advice. Based upon the facts provided and barring any 
other interest, Supervisor Peters may take part in the decisions. 
 
 
2 While the NSP will require a master plan amendment, we look to the distance between the official’s property 
and the NSP where a master plan amendment implicates only the property within a specific plan area. (See Travis 
Advice Letter, No. A-20-001.). To the extent that the master plan amendment implicates property outside the NSP plan, 
Supervisor Peter should seek additional advice. 


File No. A-20-038 
Page No. 5 
 
 
 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
Dave Bainbridge 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Counsel  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Kevin Cornwall 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division 
 
KMC:aja

sections.question:
Under the Act, may Susan Peters, a member of the Sacramento County Board of 
Supervisors, take part in decisions relating to the NewBridge Specific Plan, which would ultimately 
establish a new community and introduce new residential units, commercial space, an elementary 
school, and a fire station, given that Supervisor Peters owns real property and a commercial 
building with tenants located more than 10,000 feet away?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, given the significant distance between her economic interests and the nearest boundary 
of the NewBridge Specific Plan, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to theproject would have a material financial effect on Supervisor Peters’ real property, business, or 
source-of-income economic interests.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act provides, “[n]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to

thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.

thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(c) Any source of income aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made.

QUESTION: Under the Act, may Susan Peters, a member of the Sacramento County Board of 
Supervisors, take part in decisions relating to the NewBridge Specific Plan, which would ultimately 
establish a new community and introduce new residential units, commercial space, an elementary 
school, and a fire station, given that Supervisor Peters owns real property and a commercial 
building with tenants located more than 10,000 feet away?

CONCLUSION: Yes, given the significant distance between her economic interests and the nearest boundary 
of the NewBridge Specific Plan, it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions relating to theproject would have a material financial effect on Supervisor Peters’ real property, business, or 
source-of-income economic interests.
